blurb: A low, stoloniferous native evergreen with holly-like leaves that flush bronze-purple in winter, cheerful yellow flowers in spring, and clusters of tart blue berries in fall — one of the most versatile and tough ground-covering shrubs for Front Range shade or sun.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
spread: Spreads by stolons (trailing stems that root along the ground) into a colony; slow but steady — contains easily at a bed edge
seasons: Yellow flower clusters Apr–May; dusty blue-purple berries ripen Sep–Oct; leaves bronze-purple in winter cold, dark green in summer
wildlife: Berries eaten by birds (robins, thrushes, waxwings) and small mammals; flowers attract early native bees; dense stems provide bird cover
toxic: Berries tart/edible (used for jelly); bark and roots contain berberine which is toxic in large quantities — caution with children

care.hardiness: USDA zones 4b–9b — fully cold-hardy through Boulder's 5b–6a winters and beyond. Native across Colorado's foothills, montane, and plains zones. The evergreen foliage may scorch in exposed, windy winter sites but re-greens reliably in spring. In very exposed locations, a north or east-facing aspect or light shade from buildings reduces winter desiccation.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring (April–May) or early fall (September). Early fall planting works well: roots establish before freeze-up, and the plant comes out of dormancy vigorously the following spring. Dig the hole no deeper than the root ball and 2× as wide. In clay soils, avoid amending the backfill heavily — a sharp transition from amended soil to native clay can trap water; instead, break up the native soil with a fork. Water in thoroughly. In containers, use a well-draining potting mix and a large enough pot (≥5 gal) for the spreading habit; creeping Oregon grape is best as a permanent in-ground plant, but container specimens overwinter reliably if the pot is large or moved to a sheltered spot — roots in small containers are more cold-vulnerable than in-ground roots.[1][2]
care.sun: Remarkably adaptable — full sun to full shade. In full sun with good soil moisture it grows more densely and berries more heavily; in deep shade under conifers it spreads as a sparse but effective groundcover. At Boulder's high-altitude, intense sun, some afternoon shade extends the life of the foliage and reduces winter scorch. Avoid extreme south- or west-facing exposures with reflected heat and no irrigation.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable to a range of soils — sandy, loam, or light clay — as long as drainage is adequate. Prefers acidic to slightly acidic soil (ideally below pH 7.0); leaves develop chlorosis (yellow with green veins) in alkaline soils, which is a real risk with Boulder's native clay that commonly runs pH 7.5–8.0+. If chlorosis appears, apply sulfur to acidify the root zone or use chelated iron. Tolerates lean soil well; very fertile, constantly moist soil tends to encourage overly lush growth and root disease. A 2–3 in. wood-chip mulch conserves moisture and keeps the shallow roots cool.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate — once established, creeping Oregon grape is drought-tolerant and survives on Boulder's natural rainfall through most of the growing season. During establishment (first 1–2 growing seasons), water deeply every 5–7 days. Established plants benefit from a deep soak every 2–3 weeks in summer heat; skip if there has been significant rain. Do not keep soil consistently wet — good drainage is important.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants 2–3 ft apart for a groundcover that fills in within 3–5 years. Individual plants spread 2–5 ft wide via suckers; closer spacing (18–24 in.) fills gaps faster. Allow 3 ft from paths and bed edges so spread can be contained with annual edge-cutting rather than digging.[1][2]
care.propagation: By division: the easiest method — dig rooted suckers from the colony edge in early spring or early fall, ensure each piece has roots, and replant immediately. By stem cuttings: take semi-hardwood cuttings (4–6 in.) in summer, wound the base, dip in rooting hormone, and root in moist, gritty mix with bottom heat; success is moderate. By seed: remove the blue pulp from ripe berries (fall), clean the seeds, and sow outdoors in fall or cold-stratify for 60–90 days (moist, 34–38°F) before indoor spring sowing; germination is slow and variable. Division of suckers is by far the most practical method for the home gardener.[1][2]
care.bloom: April–May on the Front Range — bright yellow flowers in upright clusters (racemes), mildly fragrant, among the first native shrubs to bloom. An important early nectar source. Blue-purple, grape-like berries ripen in September–October; tart but edible, excellent for jelly and syrup.[1][2]
care.feeding: Minimal — a light top-dressing of compost in spring every other year is sufficient. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers that push soft, disease-prone growth. In chlorotic soils, use chelated iron or soil acidifier per label directions rather than a general fertilizer.[1]
care.maintenance: Low. Remove dead or winter-damaged canes in early spring. Cut back overly long stems or edge the colony annually to contain spread. No formal pruning shape is needed — the natural, informal mounding habit is the appeal. Remove suckers that escape the desired planting area by cutting at ground level or digging with a flat spade. In windy, exposed sites, a loose evergreen bough laid over the planting in November–December protects foliage from winter desiccation.[1]
care.selfsow: Spreads primarily by stolons — trailing stems that root where they contact soil — rather than by seed. New shoots arise along the spreading stems. The spread is slow and easily managed with a spade or annual edge-cutting at the bed perimeter. Does not self-sow aggressively by seed in the garden.[1]
care.harvest: Pick blue berries in September–October once fully ripe (deep blue-purple, slightly soft). They are tart raw but excellent cooked — rich in pectin and antioxidants. Use for jelly, syrup, or wine. Wear gloves when working around the spiny leaves.[1][3]
care.troubles: Few significant problems. Leaf scorch (brown or dry leaf tips) in winter from cold, dry wind — provide shelter or accept it as cosmetic and wait for spring re-greening. Iron chlorosis in strongly alkaline soil; acidify the root zone with sulfur. Rust (*Puccinia* spp.) can cause orange pustules on the underside of leaves, especially where wheat is grown nearby (creeping Oregon grape is an alternate host for wheat stem rust) — remove infected leaves and avoid planting near grain crops. Aphids, scale, and whitefly occasionally appear; treat with a strong water spray or insecticidal soap if populations are large.[1]
care.companions: Douglas fir, ponderosa pine, native oaks, red-twig dogwood, wood's rose, and shade-tolerant native perennials like columbine (*Aquilegia coerulea*) and wild ginger. Pairs well with kinnikinnick for a layered native groundcover planting in dry shade.[2]

edible.summary: The tart blue berries are edible — a well-documented native food, usually cooked into jelly or syrup — but the bark and roots contain berberine and are not food.[1][3]
edible.parts: Only the ripe blue-purple berries are eaten. They are a traditional Western and Plains food (eaten by the Blackfoot, Cheyenne, Flathead, Kutenai, and others) and make excellent jelly, syrup, and wine; they are tart and seedy but high in pectin. The yellow inner bark and roots are bright with berberine and were used medicinally (a traditional 'barberry' remedy), not as food.[3][1]
edible.preparation: Pick the berries fully ripe in fall; they are intensely tart and seedy raw, so they are almost always cooked and sweetened into jelly, syrup, or wine, or strained for juice. Use the berries only — do not eat the bark or roots. Wear gloves when harvesting around the spiny holly-like leaves.[1][3]
edible.caution: The berries are edible, but the bark and roots contain berberine, an alkaloid that is bitter and can be toxic in large quantities (gastrointestinal upset; not for use in pregnancy) — these parts are a folk medicine, not food, so eat only the berries and keep children from chewing the roots or stems. The spiny leaf margins are a physical hazard. Be sure of identification: confirm true creeping Oregon grape (Berberis/Mahonia repens) before eating, and never eat berries from a plant you cannot positively identify.[1][3]
